slides.title: AI Agents in 2026
slides.subtitle: A Practical Guide for Developers — Building Production-Ready Agentic Systems
slides.body: Targeted at developers with agent experience. We'll cover architecture, design patterns, frameworks, and the hard lessons from shipping agents at scale. April 2026.

slides.title: Why This Matters for Developers Right Now
bullets: Agents have moved from prototypes to production in 2026
Automate dev workflows: code gen → testing → deployment
Key 2026 shift: single → multi-agent + stateful systems
40%+ of enterprise apps to include task-specific agents (Gartner)
Ready to ship agents surviving real users, not just demos?

slides.title: Evolution: From Chatbots to Agentic Systems
left: Agents as impressive demos, not production
right: Full agentic systems: planning + memory + tools
From chat sessions to autonomous pipelines

slides.title: What Exactly Is an AI Agent? (Technical View)
bullets: Core: LLM + Tools + Memory + Planner + Executor in a loop
2026 key: stateful + controllable, not just reactive
Think: a state machine where the LLM is the decision engine
Memory: in-context, vector DB, episodic checkpoints
Tools: APIs, code exec, browser, file I/O, MCP services

slides.title: Core Agent Design Patterns in 2026
left: ReAct: Reason + Act + Observe — still foundational
right: MCP (Model Context Protocol): universal tool standard
Human-in-the-Loop: approval gates for critical steps
Eval-Driven: agents self-grade and retry on failure

slides.title: How AI Agents Actually Work (The Reasoning Loop)
slides.code:
# Agent reasoning loop — LangGraph style
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
from langgraph.checkpoint.memory import MemorySaver

def agent_node(state):
    response = llm.invoke(state["messages"])          # Think
    next_step = "tools" if response.tool_calls else END
    return {"messages": [response], "next": next_step} # Act

def tool_node(state):
    results = execute_tools(state["messages"][-1].tool_calls)  # Observe
    return {"messages": results, "next": "agent"}

graph = StateGraph(AgentState)
graph.add_node("agent", agent_node)
graph.add_node("tools", tool_node)
graph.add_edge("tools", "agent")                    # loop back
graph.add_conditional_edges("agent", lambda s: s["next"])
graph.set_entry_point("agent")

# Compile with persistence — enables time-travel debugging
app = graph.compile(checkpointer=MemorySaver())

slides.title: Top Frameworks & Tools Developers Are Using in 2026
Real-World Developer Use Cases That Deliver
left: Autonomous code review + PR agent workflows
40-50% LLM call reduction via state management
right: Research agents with live web + tool access

slides.title: Code-First Quickstart: Your First Agent in 5 Minutes
slides.code:
# pip install langgraph langchain-anthropic
from langgraph.prebuilt import create_react_agent
from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic
from langchain_community.tools import DuckDuckGoSearchRun

# Step 1: LLM + tools
llm   = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-sonnet-4-6")
tools = [DuckDuckGoSearchRun()]

# Step 2: Build agent (ReAct loop built-in)
agent = create_react_agent(llm, tools)

# Step 3: Run with persistent thread memory
config = {"configurable": {"thread_id": "session-1"}}
result = agent.invoke(
    {"messages": [{"role": "user",
                   "content": "Best LangGraph patterns in 2026?"}]},
    config=config,
)
# Pro tip: swap create_react_agent for StateGraph for full control
# Next: add MCP tools, LangSmith tracing, then scale to multi-agent

slides.title: Challenges, Best Practices & The 2026+ Future
left: Cost: loops + retries = runaway spend if unchecked
Governance: who owns agent decisions in prod?
Security: tool access + prompt injection risks
Observability: tracing multi-agent flows is hard
right: Monitor everything: costs, latency, error rates
Agents don't replace devs — they multiply us
